IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper targets an important practical problem—jointly selecting salient content and predicting discourse relations in meeting dialogues—and demonstrates clear empirical gains on standard corpora (AMI and ICSI), including a latent-discourse variant that reduces reliance on costly annotations. Results show joint learning/inference outperform separate and SVM baselines and that features from the model materially improve prediction of consistency of understanding, significantly beating the Kim and Shah (2016) state-of-the-art. These contributions are likely to influence work on dialogue summarization and discourse modeling and open meaningful follow-up directions (latent discourse, joint inference), though assumptions like requiring an attachment structure and remaining parsing challenges limit immediate transformational impact. | B(4): This paper tackles the important problem of jointly modeling content selection and discourse relations in meeting dialogues and provides a practical joint/latent modeling approach. The empirical results — joint learning and inference outperforming separate learners and SVM baselines on AMI and ICSI, plus features that improve and beat the prior state-of-the-art on consistency-of-understanding prediction — indicate the findings should change how researchers approach integrated content–discourse modeling. The work is likely to open meaningful follow-up work (e.g., latent discourse learning, joint inference for summarization) and be built upon, though it stops short of being transformative given dataset and annotation assumptions noted in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper targets an important practical problem—jointly selecting salient content and predicting discourse relations in meeting dialogues—and demonstrates clear empirical gains on standard corpora (AMI and ICSI), including a latent-discourse variant that reduces reliance on costly annotations. Results show joint learning/inference outperform separate and SVM baselines and that features from the model materially improve prediction of consistency of understanding, significantly beating the Kim and Shah (2016) state-of-the-art. These contributions are likely to influence work on dialogue summarization and discourse modeling and open meaningful follow-up directions (latent discourse, joint inference), though assumptions like requiring an attachment structure and remaining parsing challenges limit immediate transformational impact.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important problem of jointly modeling content selection and discourse relations in meeting dialogues and provides a practical joint/latent modeling approach. The empirical results — joint learning and inference outperforming separate learners and SVM baselines on AMI and ICSI, plus features that improve and beat the prior state-of-the-art on consistency-of-understanding prediction — indicate the findings should change how researchers approach integrated content–discourse modeling. The work is likely to open meaningful follow-up work (e.g., latent discourse learning, joint inference for summarization) and be built upon, though it stops short of being transformative given dataset and annotation assumptions noted in the summary.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a joint modeling perspective that simultaneously performs phrase-based content selection and discourse relation prediction in meeting dialogues, and further offers a latent-variable variant to avoid requiring gold discourse annotations—this is a clear methodological contribution beyond treating the tasks separately. It differentiates itself from prior work (e.g., Kim and Shah 2016) and shows empirical gains on AMI and ICSI, indicating the joint approach is a non-trivial extension rather than a trivial reuse. At the same time, the underlying techniques (log-linear model, SampleRank learning, message-passing and ILP inference) are established methods applied in a novel combination for this problem, so the contribution is notable but not a radical new paradigm. | B(4): The paper proposes a joint model that simultaneously performs phrase-based content selection and discourse relation prediction in meeting dialogues and also a variant treating discourse relations as latent variables so it can be trained without gold discourse annotations—an idea that goes beyond separate classifiers used in prior work. While the modeling components (log-linear features, SampleRank learning, alternating inference with message passing and ILP) reuse established techniques, their combination for joint content–discourse modeling and the demonstrated gains (including improved COU prediction over Kim and Shah 2016) represent a notable and well-differentiated contribution rather than a purely incremental tweak.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a joint modeling perspective that simultaneously performs phrase-based content selection and discourse relation prediction in meeting dialogues, and further offers a latent-variable variant to avoid requiring gold discourse annotations—this is a clear methodological contribution beyond treating the tasks separately. APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): The paper addresses core NLP/ML problems—joint content selection and discourse relation prediction in meeting dialogues—using standard modeling (log-linear joint model, latent-variable variant) and training/inference techniques (SampleRank, message-passing, ILP) appropriate for a top venue. Evaluation uses established datasets (AMI, ICSI), relevant baselines including a state-of-the-art comparison (Kim and Shah 2016), and standard metrics (F1, ROUGE), indicating it meets community expectations. The problem formulation, audience, and positioning (thorough related work, explicit limitations) align well with top-tier NLP/ML conferences. | B(5): This paper addresses discourse analysis and content selection in meeting dialogues—topics squarely within top NLP/ML venues—and proposes a joint log-linear model with appropriate learning/inference techniques (SampleRank, message passing, ILP). It evaluates on standard corpora (AMI, ICSI) against sensible baselines (SVMs, Kim & Shah 2016) using standard metrics (F1, ROUGE), and discusses limitations such as reliance on gold attachment structure. Overall the methods, datasets, evaluation, and related-work positioning indicate a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper appears well organized with a logical flow from model description to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated clearly early (joint model, latent-DR variant, and COU improvements). The methodology and experimental setup are described concretely (model formulation, learning/inference, datasets, baselines, metrics), suggesting notation and presentation are generally consistent. However, the summary does not mention figures/tables or example outputs and hyperparameter reporting/code availability is missing, which are minor presentation gaps that prevent a top score. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from problem statement to method and experiments — the joint model, learning (SampleRank), and inference procedures are described and the main contributions are stated early. Notation and model description appear consistent (a log-linear/CRF-like formulation) and the writing quality is noted as good with thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation issues remain: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and it’s not clear how effective figures/tables are; the strong assumption about given attachment structure is stated as a limitation and should be highlighted in presentation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing the joint model (and a latent-discourse variant) to appropriate baselines (SVMs, Separate-Learn, and prior work by Kim & Shah) on multiple datasets (AMI, ICSI), and by reported improvements on phrase selection, discourse prediction, and COU. The modeling and learning choices (log-linear joint model, SampleRank learning, alternating inference with message-passing and ILP) are technically sound and no incorrect mathematical claims are indicated in the summary. Minor technical concerns remain: the method assumes gold attachment structure during training and testing (a strong assumption that limits applicability), and reproducibility is weakened by lack of reported hyperparameters or released code. These issues do not, however, appear to invalidate the main experimental conclusions. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on AMI and ICSI showing the joint model (and a latent-discourse variant) outperforms separate and SVM baselines and improves COU prediction over Kim & Shah (2016). The modeling approach and learning/inference procedures are plausible and appropriate, and experiments use reasonable baselines including a prior state-of-the-art. However, the paper assumes gold attachment structure is available at train/test time and does not report hyperparameter selection or statistical significance details, which are notable but minor issues that do not undermine the primary results.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments comparing the joint model (and a latent-discourse variant) to appropriate baselines (SVMs, Separate-Learn, and prior work by Kim & Shah) on multiple datasets (AMI, ICSI), and by reported improvements on phrase selection, discourse prediction, and COU. The modeling and learning choices (log-linear joint model, SampleRank learning, alternating inference with message-passing and ILP) are technically sound and no incorrect mathematical claims are indicated in the summary. Minor technical concerns remain: the method assumes gold attachment structure during training and testing (a strong assumption that limits applicability), and reproducibility is weakened by lack of reported hyperparameters or released code. These issues do not, however, appear to invalidate the main experimental conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on AMI and ICSI showing the joint model (and a latent-discourse variant) outperforms separate and SVM baselines and improves COU prediction over Kim & Shah (2016). The modeling approach and learning/inference procedures are plausible and appropriate, and experiments use reasonable baselines including a prior state-of-the-art. SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper runs experiments on multiple datasets (AMI and ICSI, with FULL/SUB variants), compares against a wide set of baselines (SVMs, Separate-Learn, Kim and Shah 2016, DA baselines) and evaluates both content selection and discourse prediction plus downstream COU prediction, which supports the main claims. However, the summary does not report any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses, and it explicitly lacks hyperparameter reporting and code availability, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (need for labeled discourse data, reliance on given attachment structure) are acknowledged. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but has minor but important gaps in analysis and reproducibility documentation. | B(4): Experiments are broad and comparative: evaluated on multiple corpora (AMI, ICSI and variants), compared against several baselines (SVMs, Separate-Learn, Kim and Shah 2016) and measured with accuracy, F1 and ROUGE; the paper also validates downstream utility by improving consistency-of-understanding prediction. The paper includes appropriate ablations/variants (joint model vs separate learning and a latent-discourse variant) showing the benefit of joint modeling. Coverage of related work is described as thorough and limitations (need for discourse labels, difficulty of automatic discourse parsing, and the assumption that attachment structure is given) are explicitly discussed. Reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter reporting and no mention of released code, which prevents full replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper runs experiments on multiple datasets (AMI and ICSI, with FULL/SUB variants), compares against a wide set of baselines (SVMs, Separate-Learn, Kim and Shah 2016, DA baselines) and evaluates both content selection and discourse prediction plus downstream COU prediction, which supports the main claims. However, the summary does not report any ablation studies or sensitivity analyses, and it explicitly lacks hyperparameter reporting and code availability, which weakens reproducibility. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several limitations (need for labeled discourse data, reliance on given attachment structure) are acknowledged. Overall the empirical evaluation is strong but has minor but important gaps in analysis and reproducibility documentation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines—including SVM-based classifiers, Separate-Learn, n-gram SVM, and the state-of-the-art Kim and Shah (2016) HMM—on the AMI and ICSI datasets using accuracy, F1 and ROUGE metrics. Comparisons appear fair (same data and evaluation metrics) and the authors analyze differences such as joint vs separate learning and the impact of joint features (including improvements on COU). The summary does not report hyperparameter/code details and does not explicitly note cases where prior methods perform comparably or better, though related work coverage is described as thorough. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (SVM-based classifiers, Separate-Learn, n-gram SVM, majority/random baselines, and the prior SOTA Kim & Shah (2016) HMM) and evaluates on standard meeting datasets (AMI, ICSI) with common metrics (Accuracy, F1, ROUGE), indicating fair experimental conditions. It also explicitly contrasts joint versus separate learning and shows improvements, and the related work coverage is described as thorough. Minor omissions reduce completeness: hyperparameters/code are not reported in the summary, and it is not stated whether the paper acknowledges cases where prior methods perform comparably or better.

main_claims: Propose a joint model that simultaneously performs phrase-based content selection and discourse relation prediction in meeting dialogues.
Present a variant of the model that treats discourse relations as latent variables to allow training without gold-standard discourse annotations.
Show that joint learning and joint inference outperform separate learning and SVM-based baselines on phrase selection and discourse relation prediction on AMI and ICSI datasets.
Demonstrate that features derived from the joint model improve prediction of consistency of understanding (COU) and significantly outperform the state-of-the-art method of Kim and Shah (2016).
_summary.methodology: A log-linear joint model (CRF-like) over candidate phrases and discourse relations using content-only, discourse-only, and joint content-discourse features; parameters are learned via a SampleRank-based algorithm and prediction uses alternating optimization with message-passing for discourse inference and integer linear programming for phrase selection.
baselines: Kim and Shah (2016) Hidden Markov Model with discourse and head gesture features
limitations: Acquiring labeled training data for discourse relations is time-consuming.
Automatic discourse parsing in dialogues is still a challenging problem.
The model assumes the attachment structure between discourse units is given during both training and testing.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - discourse analysis and content selection in meeting dialogues
